stem: A polling organization wants to estimate the proportion of voters in a city who support a particular ballot measure. They survey a random sample of 400 voters and find that 240 support the measure. The organization reports that they are 95% confident that between 55% and 65% of all voters in the city support the measure. Which of the following best explains what this confidence interval means?

choices.label: A
choices.text: There is a 95% probability that between 55% and 65% of the 400 voters surveyed support the measure.

choices.label: B
choices.text: 95% of all possible samples of 400 voters would produce a sample proportion between 55% and 65%.

choices.label: C
choices.text: If the same sampling method were used many times, 95% of the resulting confidence intervals would contain the true proportion of all voters who support the measure.

choices.label: D
choices.text: There is a 95% probability that the true proportion of all voters who support the measure is between 55% and 65%.

explanation: A 95% confidence interval means that if we repeated the same sampling process many times and calculated a confidence interval each time, about 95% of those intervals would contain the true population parameter. The confidence level refers to the long-run behavior of the method, not the probability that any specific interval contains the true value. In this case, we know the sample proportion is $\frac{240}{400} = 0.60$ or 60%, and the confidence interval [55%, 65%] was constructed around this sample statistic using methods that capture the true population proportion 95% of the time when applied repeatedly.

distractorRationale.A: This incorrectly interprets the confidence interval as describing the sample data we already have, rather than making an inference about the population.
distractorRationale.B: This confuses the confidence interval with the sampling distribution of sample proportions, which would be centered around the true population proportion.
distractorRationale.D: This is the most common misconception - treating the confidence interval as a probability statement about the parameter, when the parameter is fixed and the interval is random.
